title: GitHub - Helldez/BigMoeOnEdge: Run a 120B-parameter MoE (60 GB) on a 12 GB phone. CPU-only, lossless, on stock llama.cpp
excerpt: Run a 120B-parameter MoE (60 GB) on a 12 GB phone. CPU-only, lossless, on stock llama.cpp - Helldez/BigMoeOnEdge
content_html:
Run Mixture-of-Experts models far bigger than your edge device's RAM.

The result: a ~60 GB model on a 12 GB phone: 1.3 tok/s lossless, byte-identical to running from RAM, 2.2 tok/s with one speed knob. CPU-only, through llama.cpp's public API.

A Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model is made of many small "experts", and each generated token only uses a few of them. BigMoeOnEdge takes that literally: it keeps the small always-needed parts of the model at hand and reads just the experts each token asks for, directly from flash storage, at the moment they're needed. The rest of the model stays on disk. That's what makes large open MoE models (Qwen3, Qwen3.6, Gemma, gpt-oss and most of their relatives) runnable on edge devices whose RAM they exceed several times over. The focus today is phones, where memory is tightest.

The flagship case: gpt-oss-120b, a ~60 GB model (Q4_K_M), on a phone with 12 GB of RAM. That's about five times more model than memory, so holding it resident is simply impossible. It runs anyway, at 1.3 tok/s with the model's own settings (2.2 tok/s with one speed knob), against 0.09 tok/s for the same file loaded the ordinary way (mmap).

gpt-oss-120b (~60 GB) on a 12 GB phone — real time, not sped up. Full three-model demo below.

bmoe_demo_combined.mp4

Left to right: gpt-oss-120b (~60 GB), Qwen3-30B-A3B (18.5 GB), Gemma-4-26B-A4B (17 GB) — recorded in the demo app on the OnePlus 15R, real time, not sped up.

And it's all plain CPU inference. No GPU, no NPU, no special hardware: four CPU cores, the phone's flash storage, and nothing else. The entire budget is a phone's UFS storage, a fraction of the bandwidth a desktop NVMe drive offers, which is exactly why the expert cache, the dense-weight policy and the read/compute overlap all have to earn their keep.

The streaming path works entirely through llama.cpp's public API, so following new llama.cpp releases is a routine submodule bump, not a merge. The one exception is the optional --overlap flag, which needs a per-expert wait point inside the CPU MoE kernel that no public API exposes: it carries a single ~25-line hook, as one commit on the fork branch bmoe/expert-ready-hook. The hook is zero-cost when unregistered, everything else builds against stock upstream, and it is dropped the moment upstream ships an equivalent. Details in docs/seam.md.

Highlights:

  • gpt-oss-120b (Q4_K_M), ~5× device RAM: 1.3 tok/s at the model's own routing width against 0.09 tok/s for the same file loaded the ordinary way (mmap), a 14× difference at matched settings. 2.2 tok/s with the one lossy knob on (fewer experts).
  • Lossless on models past RAM: Qwen3-30B-A3B (Q4_K_M, 18.5 GB) up to 5.2 tok/s, Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (Q4_K_M, 22.3 GB) up to 5.0 tok/s and Gemma-4-26B-A4B (Q4_K_M, 17.0 GB) up to 4.1 tok/s on the same phone, output identical to the resident model.
  • The problem only phones have: Android's reclaim keeps taking back the always-used weights mid-generation. --dense-weights anon feature puts them where reclaim can't cheaply take them, and it's worth 3.2× on gpt-oss-120b on its own.
  • Easy to extend: a new MoE architecture is one row in a registry, and nothing about a specific model is hardcoded in the streaming path. Because the engine sits on stock llama.cpp instead of replacing it, quantization formats, tokenizers and chat templates come for free: MXFP4 and Q4_K_M stream through the same code, since the per-expert stride is read from the model file at runtime.

About the numbers. Measured on one device (OnePlus 15R: 12 GB RAM, 11.3 GB usable, UFS 4.x storage - ...imagine with UFS 5.x...) over adb shell, 256-token greedy decode. Each number is the best observed for that configuration, and rows in a table can come from different benchmark sessions. Phone throughput moves a lot with device state (heat, free memory), so the same command can read lower. Full method and distributions: docs/benchmarks.md.

Prior art, credited: this is an engineering package of ideas from AirLLM, Apple's LLM in a flash, FlexGen, PowerInfer and EdgeMoE, not a novel technique. The closest recent work is flash-moe: a purpose-built Metal engine that streams a 397B MoE from SSD on Apple Silicon, and on an iPhone through a community fork. BigMoeOnEdge takes the other side of that problem: CPU-only, on Android, on llama.cpp's public API (bar one optional ~25-line hook, above), across architectures. See docs/limitations.md.

Try it on your phone

No build needed. Install the APK from the latest release, open the Get a model card, and tap one of the catalog entries — Qwen3-30B-A3B (~18.6 GB), Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (~22.3 GB) or Gemma-4-26B-A4B (~17 GB), each past most phones' RAM. When the download finishes, pick the model and chat; the telemetry panel shows tok/s and the compute-vs-flash split live, and every streaming knob below is in Settings.

The flagship gpt-oss-120b ships from Hugging Face as two shards, so it needs a one-time merge and a manual copy to the device: steps in the Android example README.

Features

  • Expert streaming (--moe-stream): reads only the experts each token routes to, straight from flash.
  • Expert cache (--cache-mb N|auto): keeps the most-used experts in RAM, sized by hand or to the device; the biggest lever when the model's working set fits.
  • Direct flash reads (--io-threads N): parallel read lanes that bypass the OS page cache.
  • Dense-weight policy (--dense-weights mmap|warm|anon): how the always-used (non-expert) weights are held in memory. The decisive setting for models far past RAM: on gpt-oss-120b it's worth 3.2× on its own.
  • I/O–compute overlap (--overlap): hides flash latency behind compute. Byte-identical; needs a small optional add-on to llama.cpp (see docs/seam.md).
  • Turbo top-k (--n-expert-used N): the one lossy knob. Fewer experts per token, ~+22–24% speed, output quality is yours to judge.
  • Multi-turn sessions and live telemetry: the model stays loaded across chat turns, and every run can emit a per-token breakdown of where the time went.
  • Android demo app (examples/android): a chat app with a live telemetry panel and every knob above exposed in Settings.

Supported models

Architecture Reference models Notes
qwen3moe Qwen3-30B-A3B and siblings Shipped default, validated below
qwen35moe Qwen3.6-35B-A3B and siblings Hybrid attention/SSM stack; routed experts stream unchanged
qwen2moe Qwen2 MoE family Same layout as qwen3moe
gemma4 Gemma 4 MoE (e.g. 26B-A4B) Fused expert layout, handled by its registry row
gpt-oss OpenAI gpt-oss-20b / 120b Purely routed; MXFP4 weights stream unchanged
lfm2moe Liquid AI LFM2 / LFM2.5 MoE (e.g. 8B-A1B) Hybrid conv/attention stack with leading dense blocks; those stay resident

Adding an architecture is one row in the registry; expert counts and layouts are discovered from the model file at runtime. Procedure: docs/adding-a-model.md. bmoe-cli --list-archs prints the compiled-in set.

Benchmarks

All figures are measured, not modelled. Device and protocol are in the note above; all models are Q4_K_M. How to read the tables: each row is one configuration of the same model on the same phone.

  • Configuration: the streaming settings used. mmap baseline is the same file loaded the ordinary way (no streaming), which is what the streamed rows are compared against. k is how many experts each token routes to — for us the number of experts, i.e. n_expert_used (set with --n-expert-used). Each table shows the model's default width and, where measured, a reduced k, the one lossy setting — see Turbo top-k — the one lossy option below.
  • tok/s: generation speed; higher is better.
  • Flash/token: data read from storage per generated token; lower means the cache is working.
  • Cache hit: share of expert reads served from RAM instead of flash.

Bold marks the best configuration for that model.

gpt-oss-120b (Q4_K_M) — ~60 GB on a 12 GB phone

Configuration tok/s Flash/token Cache hit
streamed, k=2, cache 2000 MiB, 8 lanes 2.2 590 MiB 32%
streamed, k=2, no cache, 4 lanes 1.8 909 MiB —
streamed, default k=4, cache 2000 MiB, 8 lanes 1.3 1292 MiB 27%
streamed, default k=4, no cache, 4 lanes 0.7 1817 MiB —
mmap baseline (no streaming) 0.09 — —

All streamed rows use --overlap --dense-weights anon --no-think. The setting that unlocked this model is --dense-weights anon: this far past RAM the phone keeps reclaiming the always-used weights mid-generation, and moving them out of the page cache was worth 3.2× by itself. Note that --no-think disables the model's reasoning and costs quality at k=4; the full matrix and quality notes are in docs/benchmarks-gpt-oss.md.

Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (Q4_K_M) — 22.3 GB

A hybrid attention/SSM MoE (256 experts, top-8, 41 blocks): most layers are linear attention (Gated Delta Net) rather than full attention, and the routed experts stream exactly like a plain qwen3moe. At ~2× device RAM the mmap baseline collapses into a fault storm; streaming with the dense weights kept out of the page cache (--dense-weights anon) runs it stably.

Configuration tok/s Flash/token Cache hit
mmap baseline (no streaming) 0.1 (unstable) — —
streamed, default k=8, cache 2000 MiB, 4 lanes, overlap 4.3 206 MiB 56%
streamed, default k=8, cache 3000 MiB, 4 lanes, overlap 5.0 144 MiB 65%
streamed, k=6, cache 2000 MiB, 4 lanes, overlap 5.4 137 MiB 60%
streamed, k=6, cache 3000 MiB, 4 lanes, overlap 5.8 91 MiB 68%

All streamed rows use --overlap --dense-weights anon. A larger cache is the main lossless lever (cache 3000 is worth +16% over 2000); the k=6 rows are the one lossy option (turbo top-k, below), worth a further ~16% by routing to six experts instead of eight. The lossless best here is cache 3000 at the model's own width, 5.0 tok/s — output byte-identical to the resident model.

Unlike the tables above, these Qwen3.6 figures are a single 96-token run rather than the 256-token best-of protocol — treat them as indicative, and not strictly comparable to the other models until re-measured under the full protocol.

Qwen3-30B-A3B (Q4_K_M) — 18.5 GB

Configuration tok/s Flash/token Cache hit
mmap baseline (no streaming) 2.0 (unstable) — —
streamed, default k=8, no cache, 4 lanes 1.7 1051 MiB —
streamed, default k=8, cache 2000 MiB, 4 lanes 2.4 480 MiB 53%
streamed, default k=8, cache 4000 MiB, 4 lanes 4.0 225 MiB 76%
streamed, default k=8, auto cache (capped 4000 MiB), 4 lanes, overlap 5.2 225 MiB 76%
streamed, k=6, cache 4000 MiB, 4 lanes 5.0 165 MiB 77%

Cache size is the dominant lever here, and the auto-sized cache with a ceiling (docs/cache-sizing.md) is the winning recipe. The mmap baseline averages ~2 tok/s but swings wildly token to token and evicts other apps. Turbo top-k (k=6) trades a little quality for a further +24% over the model's own width.

Gemma-4-26B-A4B (Q4_K_M) — 17.0 GB

Configuration tok/s Flash/token Cache hit
mmap baseline (no streaming) 0.4 — —
streamed, default k=8, no cache, 4 lanes 1.6 904 MiB —
streamed, default k=8, cache 2000 MiB, 4 lanes 2.2 366 MiB 58%
streamed, default k=8, cache 2000 MiB, 4 lanes, overlap 2.8 365 MiB 58%
streamed, default k=8, cache 4000 MiB, 4 lanes 4.1 144 MiB 82%
streamed, k=6, cache 4000 MiB, 4 lanes 5.0 98 MiB 83%

Gemma keeps more of itself permanently resident, so the 4000 MiB cache fits only when enough RAM is free at launch; cache 2000 + overlap is the dependable everyday setting on this device. Turbo top-k (k=6) is the fastest here (+22%) but changes the output.

Turbo top-k — the one lossy option

Every model here ships a routing width — the number of experts each token uses (8 for the Qwen and Gemma models, 4 for gpt-oss). Forcing it lower with --n-expert-used cuts both compute and flash reads; the k=6 rows folded into the tables above are that knob, measured A/B against each model's own width. It is worth +22–24% on the Qwen and Gemma models, and takes gpt-oss from 1.3 to 2.2 tok/s (k=2).

Everything else in this README changes how weights are fetched, never the math. This knob changes what the model computes: output differs from the full model and quality can degrade. Judge it on your own task before relying on it.

What to expect in the app

The tables above are a benchmark protocol over adb, not a chat session. The demo app lands close: the best gpt-oss config reads 1.9 tok/s in the app against 2.2 over adb, about 13% below. The gap is the protocol (short chat replies never fully warm the cache), not the app. The app's telemetry panel reports the same fields as the CLI, so you can see it directly. Analysis: docs/warmup-analysis.md.

Desktop

The same trick works on desktop where a model exceeds RAM (quick check: Qwen3-30B on a 14.8 GiB Windows PC streamed at 2.6 tok/s), but mobile is the target and desktop isn't tuned. If the model fits in RAM, just run it resident.

Quickstart (host)

git clone --recursive https://github.com/Helldez/BigMoeOnEdge.git
cd BigMoeOnEdge
scripts/build-host.sh

# stream a MoE model with a device-sized expert cache and 4 read lanes
build/cli/bmoe-cli -m Qwen3-30B-A3B-Q4_K_M.gguf --moe-stream \
  --cache-mb auto --cache-ceil-mb 4000 --io-threads 4 -t 4 -n 48 \
  --chatml -p "Explain MoE routing."

For a model several × device RAM, the shape that produced the gpt-oss numbers is:

build/cli/bmoe-cli -m gpt-oss-120b-Q4_K_M.gguf --moe-stream --overlap \
  --dense-weights anon --cache-mb 2000 --io-threads 8 -t 4 -n 256 \
  --chatml --no-think -p "Explain MoE routing."

The model must live on a real filesystem (on Android /data/local/tmp/..., not /sdcard). Omit --moe-stream for the plain mmap baseline. The byte-identity gates (streamed == resident) run with cd build && ctest --output-on-failure (needs python3 with the gguf package).

Quickstart (Android)

The demo app is in examples/android: build the CLI for arm64 with scripts/build-android.ps1, build the APK, push a model. Settings expose every streaming knob with a one-line note on what it does; defaults are the measured winning recipe for a model near RAM. For a model several × RAM, switch the dense-weights policy to anon and give the cache a real budget. A prebuilt debug APK is attached to each release.

How it works

The model loads file-backed, and the engine hooks llama.cpp's public evaluation callback. When a layer picks its experts for the current token, the engine fetches exactly those slices from flash just in time for the matmul, optionally caching the hottest ones and overlapping reads with compute. No llama.cpp sources are modified. Design and the exact API contract: docs/architecture.md, docs/seam.md.

Telemetry

A model streamed past RAM fails in ways that all look identical from outside: it's just slow. So every run accounts for its own time. --progress breaks each token down into flash I/O, cache management and compute, next to the cache hit rate and the bytes read; --csv adds the memory picture those numbers have to be read against. The Android app renders the same feed live while you chat, which is how you watch a phone quietly take its memory back mid-answer.

What makes it worth reading is that the engine is candid about its own numbers. Compute, in particular, isn't measured: it's whatever time is left once the measured parts come out, so it silently absorbs page faults and throttled cores. Rather than let that pass, the engine ships the counters that pull the residual apart, and marks anything it couldn't measure as unmeasured instead of quietly reporting zero.

When the per-token split isn't enough, two diagnostics go further: one records what the router asked for on every token, the other times the compute graph node by node. Both perturb the run they measure, and both say so.

Formats and schemas: docs/telemetry.md.

Documentation

docs/ is indexed by what you're trying to do: understand the design, extend it, or reproduce the measurements. Most-wanted entry points:

  • docs/architecture.md: the layer map and the llama.cpp relationship.
  • docs/adding-a-model.md: supporting a new MoE architecture.
  • docs/benchmarks.md: measured results and how they were produced.
  • docs/telemetry.md: the per-token line protocol, the CSV schema and the traces.
  • docs/android-memory.md: what reclaims the engine's memory on a phone.

License

Apache-2.0. See LICENSE.

Gemma-4-26B-A4B (Q4_K_M) — 17.0 GB Configuration tok/s Flash/token Cache hit mmap baseline (no streaming) 0.4 — — streamed, default k=8, no cache, 4 lanes 1.6 904 MiB — streamed, default k=8, cache 2000 MiB, 4 lanes 2.2 366 MiB 58% streamed, default k=8, cache 2000 MiB, 4 lanes, overlap 2.8 365 MiB 58% streamed, default k=8, cache 4000 MiB, 4 lanes 4.1 144 MiB 82% streamed, k=6, cache 4000 MiB, 4 lanes 5.0 98 MiB 83% Gemma keeps more of itself permanently resident, so the 4000 MiB cache fits only when enough RAM is free at launch; cache 2000 + overlap is the dependable everyday setting on this device. Turbo top-k (k=6) is the fastest here (+22%) but changes the output. Turbo top-k — the one lossy option Every model here ships a routing width — the number of experts each token uses (8 for the Qwen and Gemma models, 4 for gpt-oss). Forcing it lower with --n-expert-used cuts both compute and flash reads; the k=6 rows folded into the tables above are that knob, measured A/B against each model's own width. It is worth +22–24% on the Qwen and Gemma models, and takes gpt-oss from 1.3 to 2.2 tok/s (k=2). Everything else in this README changes how weights are fetched, never the math. This knob changes what the model computes: output differs from the full model and quality can degrade. Judge it on your own task before relying on it. What to expect in the app The tables above are a benchmark protocol over adb, not a chat session. The demo app lands close: the best gpt-oss config reads 1.9 tok/s in the app against 2.2 over adb, about 13% below. The gap is the protocol (short chat replies never fully warm the cache), not the app. The app's telemetry panel reports the same fields as the CLI, so you can see it directly. Analysis: docs/warmup-analysis.md. Desktop The same trick works on desktop where a model exceeds RAM (quick check: Qwen3-30B on a 14.8 GiB Windows PC streamed at 2.6 tok/s), but mobile is the target and desktop isn't tuned. If the model fits in RAM, just run it resident. Quickstart (host) git clone --recursive https://github.com/Helldez/BigMoeOnEdge.git cd BigMoeOnEdge scripts/build-host.sh # stream a MoE model with a device-sized expert cache and 4 read lanes build/cli/bmoe-cli -m Qwen3-30B-A3B-Q4_K_M.gguf --moe-stream \ --cache-mb auto --cache-ceil-mb 4000 --io-threads 4 -t 4 -n 48 \ --chatml -p "Explain MoE routing." For a model several × device RAM, the shape that produced the gpt-oss numbers is: build/cli/bmoe-cli -m gpt-oss-120b-Q4_K_M.gguf --moe-stream --overlap \ --dense-weights anon --cache-mb 2000 --io-threads 8 -t 4 -n 256 \ --chatml --no-think -p "Explain MoE routing." The model must live on a real filesystem (on Android /data/local/tmp/..., not /sdcard). Omit --moe-stream for the plain mmap baseline. The byte-identity gates (streamed == resident) run with cd build && ctest --output-on-failure (needs python3 with the gguf package). Quickstart (Android) The demo app is in examples/android: build the CLI for arm64 with scripts/build-android.ps1, build the APK, push a model. Settings expose every streaming knob with a one-line note on what it does; defaults are the measured winning recipe for a model near RAM. For a model several × RAM, switch the dense-weights policy to anon and give the cache a real budget. A prebuilt debug APK is attached to each release. How it works The model loads file-backed, and the engine hooks llama.cpp's public evaluation callback. When a layer picks its experts for the current token, the engine fetches exactly those slices from flash just in time for the matmul, optionally caching the hottest ones and overlapping reads with compute. No llama.cpp sources are modified. Design and the exact API contract: docs/architecture.md, docs/seam.md. Telemetry A model streamed past RAM fails in ways that all look identical from outside: it's just slow. So every run accounts for its own time. --progress breaks each token down into flash I/O, cache management and compute, next to the cache hit rate and the bytes read; --csv adds the memory picture those numbers have to be read against. The Android app renders the same feed live while you chat, which is how you watch a phone quietly take its memory back mid-answer. What makes it worth reading is that the engine is candid about its own numbers. Compute, in particular, isn't measured: it's whatever time is left once the measured parts come out, so it silently absorbs page faults and throttled cores. Rather than let that pass, the engine ships the counters that pull the residual apart, and marks anything it couldn't measure as unmeasured instead of quietly reporting zero. When the per-token split isn't enough, two diagnostics go further: one records what the router asked for on every token, the other times the compute graph node by node. Both perturb the run they measure, and both say so. Formats and schemas: docs/telemetry.md. Documentation docs/ is indexed by what you're trying to do: understand the design, extend it, or reproduce the measurements. Most-wanted entry points: docs/architecture.md: the layer map and the llama.cpp relationship. docs/adding-a-model.md: supporting a new MoE architecture. docs/benchmarks.md: measured results and how they were produced. docs/telemetry.md: the per-token line protocol, the CSV schema and the traces. docs/android-memory.md: what reclaims the engine's memory on a phone. blocks.html_en: A Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model is made of many small "experts", and each generated token only uses a few of them. BigMoeOnEdge takes that literally: it keeps the small always-needed parts of the model at hand and reads just the experts each token asks for, directly from flash storage, at the moment they're needed. The rest of the model stays on disk. That's what makes large open MoE models (Qwen3, Qwen3.6, Gemma, gpt-oss and most of their relatives) runnable on edge devices whose RAM they exceed several times over. The focus today is phones, where memory is tightest.

blocks.html_en: And it's all plain CPU inference. No GPU, no NPU, no special hardware: four CPU cores, the phone's flash storage, and nothing else. The entire budget is a phone's UFS storage, a fraction of the bandwidth a desktop NVMe drive offers, which is exactly why the expert cache, the dense-weight policy and the read/compute overlap all have to earn their keep.

sentences.en: The entire budget is a phone's UFS storage, a fraction of the bandwidth a desktop NVMe drive offers, which is exactly why the expert cache, the dense-weight policy and the read/compute overlap all have to earn their keep.

blocks.html_en: The streaming path works entirely through llama.cpp's public API, so following new llama.cpp releases is a routine submodule bump, not a merge. The one exception is the optional --overlap flag, which needs a per-expert wait point inside the CPU MoE kernel that no public API exposes: it carries a single ~25-line hook, as one commit on the fork branch bmoe/expert-ready-hook. The hook is zero-cost when unregistered, everything else builds against stock upstream, and it is dropped the moment upstream ships an equivalent. Details in docs/seam.md.

blocks.html_en: • The problem only phones have: Android's reclaim keeps taking back the always-used weights mid-generation. --dense-weights anon feature puts them where reclaim can't cheaply take them, and it's worth 3.2× on gpt-oss-120b on its own.

sentences.en: The problem only phones have: Android's reclaim keeps taking back the always-used weights mid-generation. --dense-weights anon feature puts them where reclaim can't cheaply take them, and it's worth 3.2× on gpt-oss-120b on its own.
sentences.zh: 只有手机才有的问题：Android 的回收不断地收回了中世代一直使用的重量。 --dense-weights 匿名功能将它们置于回收无法廉价获取的地方，并且它本身在 gpt-oss-120b 上的价值是 3.2 倍。

blocks.html_en: • Easy to extend: a new MoE architecture is one row in a registry, and nothing about a specific model is hardcoded in the streaming path. Because the engine sits on stock llama.cpp instead of replacing it, quantization formats, tokenizers and chat templates come for free: MXFP4 and Q4_K_M stream through the same code, since the per-expert stride is read from the model file at runtime.

sentences.en: Because the engine sits on stock llama.cpp instead of replacing it, quantization formats, tokenizers and chat templates come for free: MXFP4 and Q4_K_M stream through the same code, since the per-expert stride is read from the model file at runtime.
sentences.zh: 因为引擎位于库存 llama.cpp 上而不是替换它，所以量化格式、分词器和聊天模板都是免费的：MXFP4 和 Q4_K_M 通过相同的代码进行流式传输，因为每个专家的步幅是在运行时从模型文件中读取的。

blocks.html_en: Prior art, credited: this is an engineering package of ideas from AirLLM, Apple's LLM in a flash, FlexGen, PowerInfer and EdgeMoE, not a novel technique. The closest recent work is flash-moe: a purpose-built Metal engine that streams a 397B MoE from SSD on Apple Silicon, and on an iPhone through a community fork. BigMoeOnEdge takes the other side of that problem: CPU-only, on Android, on llama.cpp's public API (bar one optional ~25-line hook, above), across architectures. See docs/limitations.md.

When the download finishes, pick the model and chat; the telemetry panel shows tok/s and the compute-vs-flash split live, and every streaming knob below is in Settings.

blocks.html_en: The flagship gpt-oss-120b ships from Hugging Face as two shards, so it needs a one-time merge and a manual copy to the device: steps in the Android example README.

sentences.en: Features
sentences.zh: 特征

blocks.html_en: • Expert streaming (--moe-stream): reads only the experts each token routes to, straight from flash.

sentences.en: Expert streaming (--moe-stream): reads only the experts each token routes to, straight from flash.
sentences.zh: 专家流（--moe-stream）：仅读取每个令牌路由到的专家，直接从闪存。

blocks.html_en: • Expert cache (--cache-mb N|auto): keeps the most-used experts in RAM, sized by hand or to the device; the biggest lever when the model's working set fits.

sentences.en: Expert cache (--cache-mb N|auto): keeps the most-used experts in RAM, sized by hand or to the device; the biggest lever when the model's working set fits.
sentences.zh: 专家缓存（--cache-mb N|auto）：将最常用的专家保存在 RAM 中，手动调整大小或根据设备调整大小；当模型的工作集适合时最大的杠杆。

blocks.html_en: • Direct flash reads (--io-threads N): parallel read lanes that bypass the OS page cache.

sentences.en: Direct flash reads (--io-threads N): parallel read lanes that bypass the OS page cache.
sentences.zh: 直接闪存读取（--io-threads N）：绕过操作系统页面缓存的并行读取通道。

blocks.html_en: • Dense-weight policy (--dense-weights mmap|warm|anon): how the always-used (non-expert) weights are held in memory. The decisive setting for models far past RAM: on gpt-oss-120b it's worth 3.2× on its own.

sentences.en: Dense-weight policy (--dense-weights mmap|warm|anon): how the always-used (non-expert) weights are held in memory.
sentences.zh: 密集权重策略（--dense-weights mmap|warm|anon）：如何将始终使用的（非专家）权重保存在内存中。

sentences.en: The decisive setting for models far past RAM: on gpt-oss-120b it's worth 3.2× on its own.
sentences.zh: 对于远远超过 RAM 的模型的决定性设置：在 gpt-oss-120b 上，它本身的价值是 3.2 倍。

blocks.html_en: • I/O–compute overlap (--overlap): hides flash latency behind compute. Byte-identical; needs a small optional add-on to llama.cpp (see docs/seam.md).

sentences.en: I/O–compute overlap (--overlap): hides flash latency behind compute.
sentences.zh: I/O-计算重叠（--overlap）：隐藏计算背后的闪存延迟。

sentences.en: Byte-identical; needs a small optional add-on to llama.cpp (see docs/seam.md).
sentences.zh: 字节相同；需要 llama.cpp 的一个小的可选附加组件（请参阅 docs/seam.md）。

blocks.html_en: • Turbo top-k (--n-expert-used N): the one lossy knob. Fewer experts per token, ~+22–24% speed, output quality is yours to judge.

sentences.en: Turbo top-k (--n-expert-used N): the one lossy knob.
sentences.zh: Turbo top-k（--n-专家使用的 N）：一个有损旋钮。

sentences.en: Fewer experts per token, ~+22–24% speed, output quality is yours to judge.
sentences.zh: 每个代币的专家更少，速度提高 22-24%，输出质量由您来判断。

blocks.html_en: • Multi-turn sessions and live telemetry: the model stays loaded across chat turns, and every run can emit a per-token breakdown of where the time went.

sentences.en: Multi-turn sessions and live telemetry: the model stays loaded across chat turns, and every run can emit a per-token breakdown of where the time went.
sentences.zh: 多回合会话和实时遥测：模型在聊天回合中保持加载状态，每次运行都可以发出每个令牌的时间消耗明细。

blocks.html_en: • Android demo app (examples/android): a chat app with a live telemetry panel and every knob above exposed in Settings.

sentences.en: Android demo app (examples/android): a chat app with a live telemetry panel and every knob above exposed in Settings.
sentences.zh: Android 演示应用程序（示例/android）：一个带有实时遥测面板的聊天应用程序，上面的每个旋钮都在“设置”中公开。

The setting that unlocked this model is --dense-weights anon: this far past RAM the phone keeps reclaiming the always-used weights mid-generation, and moving them out of the page cache was worth 3.2× by itself. blocks.html_en: Every model here ships a routing width — the number of experts each token uses (8 for the Qwen and Gemma models, 4 for gpt-oss). Forcing it lower with --n-expert-used cuts both compute and flash reads; the k=6 rows folded into the tables above are that knob, measured A/B against each model's own width. It is worth +22–24% on the Qwen and Gemma models, and takes gpt-oss from 1.3 to 2.2 tok/s (k=2).

blocks.html_en: The model loads file-backed, and the engine hooks llama.cpp's public evaluation callback. When a layer picks its experts for the current token, the engine fetches exactly those slices from flash just in time for the matmul, optionally caching the hottest ones and overlapping reads with compute. No llama.cpp sources are modified. Design and the exact API contract: docs/architecture.md, docs/seam.md.

blocks.html_en: A model streamed past RAM fails in ways that all look identical from outside: it's just slow. So every run accounts for its own time. --progress breaks each token down into flash I/O, cache management and compute, next to the cache hit rate and the bytes read; --csv adds the memory picture those numbers have to be read against. The Android app renders the same feed live while you chat, which is how you watch a phone quietly take its memory back mid-answer.

blocks.html_en: What makes it worth reading is that the engine is candid about its own numbers. Compute, in particular, isn't measured: it's whatever time is left once the measured parts come out, so it silently absorbs page faults and throttled cores. Rather than let that pass, the engine ships the counters that pull the residual apart, and marks anything it couldn't measure as unmeasured instead of quietly reporting zero.

content_html_zh:
运行远大于边缘设备 RAM 的专家混合模型。

结果：12 GB 手机上的约 60 GB 模型：1.3 tok/s 无损，字节与从 RAM 运行相同，2.2 tok/s（带一个速度旋钮）。 仅 CPU，通过 llama.cpp 的公共 API。

专家混合（MoE）模型由许多小型“专家”组成，每个生成的代币仅使用其中的几个。 BigMoeOnEdge 从字面上理解了这一点：它将模型中始终需要的小部分保留在手边，并在需要时直接从闪存存储中读取每个令牌所需的专家。 模型的其余部分保留在磁盘上。 这就是大型开放 MoE 模型（Qwen3、Qwen3.6、Gemma、gpt-oss 及其大多数同类）可以在 RAM 超出数倍的边缘设备上运行的原因。 今天的焦点是内存最紧张的手机。

旗舰机壳：gpt-oss-120b，约 60 GB 型号 (Q4_K_M)，配备 12 GB RAM 的手机。 这大约是模型的内存数量的五倍，因此将其保留在常驻位置是根本不可能的。 无论如何，它以模型自己的设置以 1.3 tok/s 的速度运行（使用一个速度旋钮为 2.2 tok/s），而以普通方式 (mmap) 加载的同一文件则为 0.09 tok/s。

12 GB 手机上的 gpt-oss-120b (~60 GB) — 实时，未加速。 下面是完整的三模型演示。

bmoe_demo_combined.mp4

从左到右：gpt-oss-120b (~60 GB)、Qwen3-30B-A3B (18.5 GB)、Gemma-4-26B-A4B (17 GB) — 在 OnePlus 15R 上的演示应用程序中实时录制，未加速。

这都是简单的 CPU 推理。 没有 GPU、没有 NPU、没有特殊硬件：四个 CPU 核心、手机闪存，仅此而已。 整个预算是手机的 UFS 存储，只是桌面 NVMe 驱动器提供的带宽的一小部分，这正是专家缓存、密集权重策略和读取/计算重叠都必须保持不变的原因。

流路径完全通过 llama.cpp 的公共 API 工作，因此以下新的 llama.cpp 版本是常规子模块碰撞，而不是合并。 一个例外是可选的 --overlap 标志，它需要 CPU MoE 内核内的每个专家等待点，没有公共 API 公开：它带有一个约 25 行的钩子，作为 fork 分支 bmoe/expert-ready-hook 上的一次提交。 当未注册时，该钩子是零成本的，其他一切都是根据上游的库存而构建的，并且在上游运送等价物时就被丢弃。 详细信息请参见 docs/seam.md。

亮点：

  • gpt-oss-120b (Q4_K_M)，~5× 设备 RAM：模型自身的路由宽度为 1.3 tok/s，而以普通方式 (mmap) 加载的同一文件为 0.09 tok/s，匹配设置下有 14× 差异。 打开一个有损旋钮时为 2.2 tok/s（专家较少）。
  • RAM 之前的型号上的无损：Qwen3-30B-A3B（Q4_K_M，18.5 GB）高达 5.2 tok/s，Qwen3.6-35B-A3B（Q4_K_M，22.3 GB）高达 5.0 tok/s，Gemma-4-26B-A4B（Q4_K_M，17.0 GB）高达 4.1 tok/s 在同一部手机上，输出与常驻型号相同。
  • 只有手机才有的问题：Android 的回收不断地收回了中世代一直使用的重量。 --dense-weights 匿名功能将它们置于回收无法廉价获取的地方，并且它本身在 gpt-oss-120b 上的价值是 3.2 倍。
  • 易于扩展：新的 MoE 架构是注册表中的一行，并且有关特定模型的任何内容都不会硬编码在流路径中。 因为引擎位于库存 llama.cpp 上而不是替换它，所以量化格式、分词器和聊天模板都是免费的：MXFP4 和 Q4_K_M 通过相同的代码进行流式传输，因为每个专家的步幅是在运行时从模型文件中读取的。
关于数字。 在一台设备上测量（OnePlus 15R：12 GB RAM、11.3 GB 可用空间、UFS 4.x 存储 - ...想象一下 UFS 5.x...） 通过 adb shell，256 个令牌贪婪解码。 每个数字都是该配置的最佳观察值，表中的行可以来自不同的基准测试会话。 电话吞吐量随设备状态（热量、可用内存）变化很大，因此相同的命令可以读取较低的数据。 完整方法和发行版：docs/benchmarks.md。
现有技术，注明：这是来自 AirLLM、Apple 的 LLM in a flash、FlexGen、PowerInfer 和 EdgeMoE 的工程创意包，不是一项新颖的技术。 最近的工作是 flash-moe：一种专门构建的 Metal 引擎，可以从 Apple Silicon 上的 SSD 传输 397B MoE，并通过社区分叉在 iPhone 上传输。 BigMoeOnEdge 解决了该问题的另一面：仅限 CPU、在 Android 上、在 llama.cpp 的公共 API 上（除了上面一个可选的 ~25 行钩子）、跨架构。 请参阅文档/限制.md。

在手机上尝试一下

无需构建。 安装最新版本的 APK，打开获取型号卡，然后点击目录条目之一 - Qwen3-30B-A3B (~18.6 GB)、Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (~22.3 GB) 或 Gemma-4-26B-A4B (~17 GB)，每个条目都超过了大多数手机的 RAM。 下载完成后，选择型号并聊天；遥测面板实时显示 tok/s 和计算与闪存的分割，下面的每个流旋钮都在“设置”中。

旗舰产品 gpt-oss-120b 作为两个分片从 Hugging Face 发货，因此需要一次性合并并手动复制到设备：Android 示例自述文件中的步骤。

特征

  • 专家流（--moe-stream）：仅读取每个令牌路由到的专家，直接从闪存。
  • 专家缓存（--cache-mb N|auto）：将最常用的专家保存在 RAM 中，手动调整大小或根据设备调整大小；当模型的工作集适合时最大的杠杆。
  • 直接闪存读取（--io-threads N）：绕过操作系统页面缓存的并行读取通道。
  • 密集权重策略（--dense-weights mmap|warm|anon）：如何将始终使用的（非专家）权重保存在内存中。 对于远远超过 RAM 的模型的决定性设置：在 gpt-oss-120b 上，它本身的价值是 3.2 倍。
  • I/O-计算重叠（--overlap）：隐藏计算背后的闪存延迟。 字节相同；需要 llama.cpp 的一个小的可选附加组件（请参阅 docs/seam.md）。
  • Turbo top-k（--n-专家使用的 N）：一个有损旋钮。 每个代币的专家更少，速度提高 22-24%，输出质量由您来判断。
  • 多回合会话和实时遥测：模型在聊天回合中保持加载状态，每次运行都可以发出每个令牌的时间消耗明细。
  • Android 演示应用程序（示例/android）：一个带有实时遥测面板的聊天应用程序，上面的每个旋钮都在“设置”中公开。

支持机型

建筑学 参考型号 Notes
qwen3moe Qwen3-30B-A3B 和兄弟姐妹 默认发货，在下面验证
qwen35萌 Qwen3.6-35B-A3B 和兄弟姐妹 混合注意力/SSM堆栈；路由专家流不变
qwen2moe Qwen2教育部家族 与 qwen3moe 相同的布局
gemma4 Gemma 4 MoE（例如 26B-A4B） 融合的专家布局，由其注册表行处理
GPT-美国 OpenAI gpt-oss-20b / 120b 纯路由； MXFP4 权重流不变
lfm2moe 液体 AI LFM2 / LFM2.5 MoE（例如 8B-A1B） 具有领先密集块的混合卷积/注意力堆栈；那些留下来的居民

添加架构只是注册表中的一行；专家计数和布局是在运行时从模型文件中发现的。 过程：docs/adding-a-model.md。 bmoe-cli --list-archs 打印编译集。

基准测试

所有数字都是测量的，而不是建模的。 设备和协议见上面注释；所有型号均为 Q4_K_M。 如何阅读表格：每一行是同一手机上同一型号的一个配置。

  • 配置：使用的流设置。 mmap 基线是以普通方式加载的同一文件（无流式传输），这是流式传输行的比较对象。 k 是每个令牌路由到的专家数量 - 对于我们来说是专家的数量，即 n_expert_used （使用 --n-expert-used 设置）。 每个表都显示了模型的默认宽度以及测量时的减小的 k、一个有损设置 — 请参阅 Turbo top-k — 下面的一个有损选项。
  • tok/s：生成速度；越高越好。
  • Flash/token：每个生成的token从存储中读取的数据；较低意味着缓存正在工作。
  • 缓存命中：从 RAM 而不是闪存提供的专家读取的份额。

粗体标记了该型号的最佳配置。

Turbo top-k — 一种有损选项

这里的每个模型都有一个路由宽度——每个代币使用的专家数量（Qwen 和 Gemma 模型为 8 个，gpt-oss 为 4 个）。 使用 --n-expert-used 强制降低它可以减少计算和闪存读取；折叠到上表中的 k=6 行是旋钮，根据每个型号自己的宽度测量 A/B。 它在 Qwen 和 Gemma 模型上的价值 +22-24%，并且 gpt-oss 从 1.3 tok/s 到 2.2 tok/s (k=2)。

本自述文件中的其他所有内容都改变了权重的获取方式，而不是数学。 该旋钮会更改模型的计算内容：输出与完整模型不同，并且质量可能会下降。 在依赖它之前，先根据自己的任务来判断它。

应用程序中的预期内容

上表是 adb 上的基准协议，而不是聊天会话。 演示应用程序非常接近：最好的 gpt-oss 配置在应用程序中读取 1.9 tok/s，而 adb 则为 2.2，大约低 13%。 差距在于协议（简短的聊天回复永远不会完全预热缓存），而不是应用程序。 该应用程序的遥测面板报告与 CLI 相同的字段，因此您可以直接看到它。 分析：docs/warmup-analysis.md。

桌面

同样的技巧也适用于模型超出 RAM 的桌面（快速检查：14.8 GiB Windows PC 上的 Qwen3-30B，以 2.6 tok/s 进行流传输），但移动设备是目标，桌面未进行调整。 如果模型适合 RAM，则只需常驻运行即可。

快速入门（主机）

git clone --recursive https://github.com/Helldez/BigMoeOnEdge.git
cd BigMoeOnEdge
scripts/build-host.sh

# stream a MoE model with a device-sized expert cache and 4 read lanes
build/cli/bmoe-cli -m Qwen3-30B-A3B-Q4_K_M.gguf --moe-stream \
  --cache-mb auto --cache-ceil-mb 4000 --io-threads 4 -t 4 -n 48 \
  --chatml -p "Explain MoE routing."

对于多个设备 RAM 的模型，生成 gpt-oss 数字的形状为：

build/cli/bmoe-cli -m gpt-oss-120b-Q4_K_M.gguf --moe-stream --overlap \
  --dense-weights anon --cache-mb 2000 --io-threads 8 -t 4 -n 256 \
  --chatml --no-think -p "Explain MoE routing."

该模型必须存在于真实的文件系统上（在 Android /data/local/tmp/... 上，而不是 /sdcard）。 对于普通 mmap 基线，省略 --moe-stream 。 字节身份门（流式==驻留）使用 cd build && ctest --output-on-failure 运行（需要带有 gguf 包的 python3）。

快速入门（安卓）

演示应用程序位于examples/android中：使用scripts/build-android.ps1构建arm64的CLI，构建APK，推送模型。 设置公开每个流媒体旋钮，并带有一行注释说明其功能；默认值是 RAM 附近模型的测量获胜秘诀。 对于几个 RAM 的模型，将密集权重策略切换为匿名，并为缓存提供真实的预算。 每个版本都附带一个预构建的调试 APK。

它是如何运作的

该模型加载文件支持，并且引擎挂钩 llama.cpp 的公共评估回调。 当某个层为当前令牌选择专家时，引擎会及时从闪存中准确获取这些切片以进行 matmul，可以选择缓存最热门的切片并与计算重叠读取。 没有修改 llama.cpp 源。 设计和确切的 API 契约：docs/architecture.md、docs/seam.md。

遥测

流过 RAM 的模型失败的方式从外部看起来都相同：只是速度慢。 所以每次运行都有自己的时间。 --progress 将每个标记分解为闪存 I/O、缓存管理和计算，以及缓存命中率和读取的字节数； --csv 添加必须读取这些数字的内存图片。 当你聊天时，Android 应用程序会实时呈现相同的提要，这就是你看到手机在回答过程中悄悄收回内存的方式。

值得一读的是，引擎对自己的数据很坦诚。 特别是计算，它是不被测量的：一旦测量的部分出来，它就剩下任何时间，因此它默默地吸收页面错误和节流核心。 引擎并没有让这种情况过去，而是运送了计数器，将残差分开，并将任何它无法测量的东西标记为未测量，而不是悄悄地报告零。

当每个令牌的分割不够时，两种诊断会更进一步：一种记录路由器对每个令牌的要求，另一种则逐个节点地计算图。 两者都干扰了他们测量的跑步，而且都这么说。

格式和模式：docs/telemetry.md。
